variables.content:
For an added-electron excitation of momentum \mathbf{p} with |\mathbf{p}|>k_F in the homogeneous two-dimensional electron gas, the leading finite-size error in the excitation energy arising from the change in the momentum distribution is dominated by the kinetic-energy term
      \delta T_{\mathbf p}^N=\frac{\hbar^2 p^2}{2m}\,Z_{\mathbf p}^N\big[e^{-\Delta_N}-1\big],
      where Z_{\mathbf p}^N is the finite-N quasiparticle weight at \mathbf{p} and \Delta_N is the missing-small-q integral defined above; in leading order the corresponding potential-energy correction is independent of \mathbf{p} and therefore does not affect the slope of the excitation dispersion used to extract the effective mass m^{*} from near-k_F fits.Eq. (12)

For the homogeneous unpolarized two-dimensional electron gas in a finite square cell, adding one electron with momentum \mathbf{p} (|\mathbf{p}|>k_F) to the N-particle ground state produces a finite-size correction to the total kinetic-energy contribution of the excitation given by
      \delta T_{\mathbf{p}}^{N}=\frac{\hbar^{2}\mathbf{p}^{2}}{2m}\;Z_{\mathbf{p}}^{N}\big[e^{-\Delta_{N}}-1\big],
      where Z_{\mathbf{p}}^{N} is the finite-N quasiparticle weight at momentum \mathbf{p} and \Delta_{N} is the missing-small-q integral defined in conclusion 1. Because \Delta_{N}\sim N^{-1/4} for large N, the leading finite-size error in the near-k_F excitation dispersion used to extract the single-particle effective mass m^{*} (via the linearized relation \varepsilon_{\mathbf{k}}\approx\hbar^{2}k_F(k-k_F)/m^{*}) also scales as N^{-1/4}; therefore, extrapolations that assume faster vanishing finite-size errors can give qualitatively incorrect conclusions about the sign and magnitude of m^{*}-m for typical accessible system sizes.

The ensemble-averaged ground-state energy per particle $E(r_s)$ computed for the disordered two-dimensional electron gas departs increasingly from the reference homogeneous-system QMC energy (the Attaccalite et al. QMC parametrization for the homogeneous 2D electron gas) as the disorder strength is increased: for impurities placed at large setback distance $d$ the computed $E(r_s)$ approaches the homogeneous QMC energy, while increasing disorder strength (either larger impurity concentration $n_i$ or smaller setback distance $d$) produces larger deviations from the homogeneous value, and these disorder-induced deviations become more pronounced at larger $r_s$ (lower average density), indicating that electron–electron interaction effects amplify the influence of disorder on the ground-state energy.

In finite periodic simulation cells for a two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas using Slater-Jastrow-backflow trial wave functions that contain variational parameters in the Jastrow and backflow factors, reoptimizing those variational parameters for an excited-state configuration (created by adding or removing an electron in a specified Bloch state) can yield a lower total DMC energy in that finite cell relative to using the ground-state-optimized parameters; because, in the thermodynamic limit, the optimal short-range correlation functions are unchanged by a single excitation, this finite-cell energy lowering is a manifestation of finite-size error and can reduce computed excitation bandwidths and therefore amplify finite-size bias in bandwidth-derived quantities such as the quasiparticle effective mass.

After applying analytic finite-size corrections and extrapolating to the thermodynamic limit for the two-dimensional electron gas in the density range $1\le r_{s}\le 10$, the single-particle effective mass is found to be enhanced relative to the bare electron mass ($m^{*}/m>1$) across this range, and the renormalization factor $Z$ obtained from the corrected Quantum Monte Carlo calculations is systematically smaller than the corresponding values obtained from perturbative random-phase approximation (RPA) calculations. These corrected QMC findings therefore indicate that perturbative RPA tends to overestimate $Z$ and underestimate the quasiparticle mass enhancement in the studied density regime, while calculations that go beyond perturbative RPA by incorporating many-body local-field (charge- and spin-density) corrections give results qualitatively consistent with the corrected QMC values.

A post-processing protocol to remove finite-size (FS) errors from AFQMC excitation energies computed in periodic simulation cells combines a one-body plus electron–hole correction and a two-body correction and then extracts quasiparticle energies by least-squares fitting: denote by \(e_{n}(k)\) the single-particle Kohn–Sham density-functional-theory (DFT) band energies obtained on a dense Brillouin-zone \(k\)-grid and by \(\Delta E_{ij}^{\mathrm{DFT}}(k)\) the DFT excitation energy computed in the same finite simulation cell and \(k\)-point as the AFQMC many-body calculation; define the one-body plus electron–hole finite-size correction \(\delta E_{ij}^{\mathrm{eh},1\mathrm{b}}(k)=e_{j}(k)-e_{i}(k)-\Delta E_{ij}^{\mathrm{DFT}}(k)\) to correct single-particle discretization and electron–hole pairing bias, and define the two-body finite-size correction \(\delta E_{ij}^{2\mathrm{b}}(k)=\Delta E_{ij}^{\mathrm{LDA}}(k)-\Delta E_{ij}^{\mathsf{FS}\text{-}\mathsf{LDA}}(k)\) as the difference between excitation energies computed in the finite cell with a standard local-density-approximation (LDA) functional and with an LDA functional parametrized for finite-size effects; the total finite-size correction is \(\delta E_{ij}(k)=\delta E_{ij}^{\mathrm{eh},1\mathrm{b}}(k)+\delta E_{ij}^{2\mathrm{b}}(k)\), which is added to the raw AFQMC excitation energies \(\Delta E_{ij}^{\mathrm{QMC}}(k)\) to form corrected excitations \(\Delta E_{ij}(k)=\Delta E_{ij}^{\mathrm{QMC}}(k)+\delta E_{ij}(k)\); quasiparticle band energies \(\epsilon_{n}(k)\) are then obtained at each \(k\) by minimizing the least-squares objective \(\min\limits_{\{\epsilon_{n}(k)\}}\sum_{i\in\mathbf{v}}\sum_{j\in\mathbf{c}}\big(\Delta E_{ij}(k)-[\epsilon_{j}(k)-\epsilon_{i}(k)]\big)^{2}\), where \(\mathbf{v}\) and \(\mathbf{c}\) denote the sets of occupied and unoccupied band indices considered, and with the highest occupied quasiparticle energy constrained to the corresponding DFT eigenvalue \(\epsilon_{m}(k)=e_{m}(k)\).

The Kwee–Zhang–Krakauer (KZK) finite-size correction constructs finite-size local-density-approximation (LDA) exchange-correlation energy functionals by performing homogeneous-electron-gas calculations at discrete electron densities and finite cell sizes and uses these finite-size LDA functionals in density-functional-theory calculations on the identical periodic supercell employed in the quantum Monte Carlo calculation to compute a postprocessing two-body correction; the effectiveness of this KZK correction for a given material therefore relies on the premise that the standard, infinite-size-extrapolated DFT-LDA provides a reasonable baseline description of the material's electronic structure so that the finite-size LDA adjustments capture the dominant finite-size QMC errors.

For the ideal (strictly two-dimensional, zero-width, disorder-free) paramagnetic two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas, the extrapolated thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass remains close to unity across the studied density parameters r_s=1, 5, and 10, and the high-precision DMC extrapolations reported here show no evidence for a strong enhancement of m^* at low density in this ideal system.

For the two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas, with the 2D coupling parameter defined by $r_{s}a_{B}=(\pi n)^{-1/2}$, the self-consistent Hartree two-electron scattering scheme reproduces available QMC pair-distribution data accurately at weak-to-moderate coupling (for example at $r_{s}=1$) but fails quantitatively at stronger coupling (for example at $r_{s}=5$): the scheme does not capture the emergence of a pronounced first-neighbor peak in $g(r)$, and discrepancies with QMC are already visible at $r_{s}=5$ and grow with increasing $r_{s}$.

For the paramagnetic two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas at density parameter r_s=10, extrapolating finite-N DMC effective masses obtained from quartic-band fits gives the thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass m^*(∞)=1.03(4) a.u., where m^* is defined by m^*=k_F/(dE/dk)_{k_F}.
